title: Knowing About God… or Actually Knowing Him?
subtitle: Intellectual and Sapiential Ways of Approaching the Living Truth
description: Intellectual and Sapiential Ways of Approaching the Living Truth
body_html:
Most Christians do not struggle because they doubt God.
They struggle because, often without realizing it, they have learned how to know about God far more fluently than how to know Him.

This is not a failure of faith. It is usually a matter of formation.

Every Christian culture, church, and community forms people toward certain ways of knowing: what is trusted, what is emphasized, what kind of language feels legitimate when speaking about God. Over time, these habits shape not only belief, but the posture of the soul toward the mystery of God Himself.

At the heart of this lies a distinction the Christian tradition has long recognized: intellectual contemplation and sapiential contemplation. These are not competing spiritualities. They do not contradict one another. But they do shape the soul differently—and they bring us to God in different ways.

Knowledge and Wisdom Are Not the Same

Scripture already names this distinction with surprising clarity:

“He made known his ways to Moses,
his acts to the people of Israel.”
— Psalm 103:7

Israel knew what God did. Moses came to know how God is.

Both involve real knowledge of God. But they are not the same kind of knowing. One is primarily informational—knowledge of God’s actions. The other is formational—knowledge of God’s ways, His inner manner of being and relating.

This is the biblical distinction between knowledge and wisdom.

Knowledge can be accumulated.
Wisdom must be received.

Intellectual Contemplation: Knowing the Truth Clearly

Intellectual contemplation (contemplatio per intellectum) is the mode of knowing most Western Christians are implicitly trained in. It values clarity, precision, definition, and coherence. Truth is apprehended by the intellect as intelligible form—seen, grasped, ordered.

This is a genuine participation in truth.

St. Thomas Aquinas exemplifies this magnificently. For Aquinas, contemplation perfects the intellect by truth. Theology is not merely analytical; it is contemplative because the intellect is elevated to behold what is true. This is why the Summa Theologiae can feel prayerful, even overwhelming, in its luminous order. The intellect is brought right up to the edge of the mystery.

And this matters. Christianity does not despise the intellect. The intellect truly does lead us to Truth.

But here the faith makes a startling claim: Truth is not a concept. Truth is a Person.

“I am the way, the truth, and the life.”
— John 14:6

The intellect can recognize Him.
It can name Him.
It can confess Him.

But it cannot contain Him.

No matter how refined, the intellect brings us to the threshold of the mystery, not into its depths. It allows us to stand rightly before Christ as Truth—but not yet to rest within Him.

Sapiential Contemplation: Wisdom That Enters the Mystery

This is where sapiential contemplation (contemplatio per sapientiam) begins.

Sapiential knowing does not reject intellectual knowledge; it presupposes it. But wisdom is knowledge that has crossed over into participation. It is not merely about seeing truth, but about being drawn into it.

Scripture names this shift with almost shocking simplicity:

“Taste and see that the Lord is good.”
— Psalm 34:8

Wisdom is a tasting. A savoring. A knowing that happens not by grasping, but by consenting.

This is why wisdom in Scripture is never merely clever or insightful. It is relational, embodied, and transformative. Wisdom does not ask only, “Is this true?” It asks, “How do I live from this?”

St. Bonaventure and Wisdom “Present in the Soul by Inherency”

Here Bonaventure gives us crucial language—language that needs unpacking.

Bonaventure distinguishes between two forms of beatitudo (blessedness):

  • Objective beatitude: God as the ultimate end of all rational activity—the goal the intellect seeks.

  • Subjective beatitude: God’s life present within the soul itself.

When Bonaventure says that beatitude is “present in the soul by inherency,” he does not mean that the soul becomes divine by nature. He means that God’s own life, by grace, comes to inhabit the soul, shaping it from within rather than merely informing it from without.

In this mode, wisdom does not simply enlighten the intellect. It actualizes the whole soul—intellect, will, desire, and affection—into a lived participation in God. Knowing and loving are no longer separable operations. They converge into what Bonaventure calls fruitio: the enjoyment of God.

This is not anti-intellectual.
It is post-possessive.

The soul no longer seeks to master the truth.
It allows itself to be mastered by Love.

From Mental Prayer to Receptive Contemplation

This is why wisdom changes the mode of prayer.

Intellectual contemplation often expresses itself through mental prayer: meditation, reflection, theological consideration. These are good and necessary. They engage the intellect actively and reverently.

But wisdom gently guides the soul to a different posture.

At a certain point, the mind is invited to let go of control, to release the need to keep understanding, explaining, or structuring the encounter. Mental prayer slowly gives way to passive contemplation—a receptive attentiveness in which the soul allows itself to be acted upon.

This is not emptiness.
It is availability.

It is receptivity to what Jesus calls Living Water:

“Whoever believes in me, as Scripture says,
‘Out of his heart shall flow rivers of living water.’”
— John 7:38

And the source of that water is not abstract.

“One of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear,
and at once there came out blood and water.”
— John 19:34

Wisdom draws the soul to rest at this hidden fountain—not to analyze it, but to drink.

Formation, Culture, and Orientation

These two modes of knowing shape entire communities.

Churches and cultures oriented primarily toward intellectual contemplation tend to emphasize explanation, doctrinal precision, and clarity. Those oriented more toward sapiential contemplation emphasize prayer, transformation, intimacy, and surrender.

Most people instinctively recognize where they feel more at home.

But preference is not the same as fullness.

Knowledge without wisdom can leave us standing reverently before Christ, yet still holding Him at arm’s length. Wisdom without truth loses its anchor. The tradition never intended a choice between them.

Share

Standing at the Threshold—or Entering In

While studying philosophy and theology at Franciscan University of Steubenville (FUS), I felt this tension personally. I read the Summa in the chapel because the truth itself felt holy. And yet, I sensed that clarity alone was not enough. The intellect had brought me to Christ—but wisdom was asking me to stop managing the encounter and start receiving it.

I had a profound dream that gave this intuition a face: centered around my view of Thomas Aquinas and Mother Teresa.

The Dream: Brilliance, Holiness, and the Shift I Didn’t Expect

I had this dream while I was taking the Thomistic Traditions class at FUS. I was deep into questions about knowledge—what it means to know God, the difference between knowing about Him and actually knowing Him, the distinction between knowledge and wisdom, even if I couldn’t quite articulate it yet.

In the dream, I was in a living room. A group of us were gathered together, seated around the room, listening.

And Thomas Aquinas was there.

He was the center of gravity in the room. The spotlight—if you could call it that—rested on him. He was speaking, teaching, sharing insight. And we were all absolutely captivated. There was a collective sense of awe at how brilliant he was—how clear, how ordered, how penetrating his mind was. Everything he said felt true. Solid. Coherent. Like truth revealing its own architecture.

As we listened, no one wanted to interrupt. No one wanted to look away. As we were all struck with awe and amazement, with an experience of revelation pouring into our souls through his words.

Then, quietly, the door opened.

(What happened next showed me the difference between brilliance and holiness—between seeing the truth and living from it.)
